question: B-6. Let \( \delta(x) \) be the greatest odd divisor of the positive integer \( x \). Show that \( \sum_{n=1}^{x} \delta(n) / n-2 x / 3 \mid<1 \), for all positive integers \( x \).
solution:
B-6 Set
\[
S(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{x} \frac{\delta(n)}{n}
\]

Note that \( \delta(2 m+1)=2 m+1, \delta(2 m)=\delta(m) \) and that \( S(2 x+1)=S(2 x)+1 \). Dividing the summation for \( S(2 x) \) into even and odd values of the index produces the following relation:
\[
S(2 x)=\sum_{m=1}^{x} \frac{\delta(2 m)}{2 m}+\sum_{m=1}^{x} \frac{\delta(2 m-1)}{2 m-1}=\frac{1}{2} S(x)+x .
\]

If we denote \( S(x)-\frac{2 x}{3} \) by \( F(x) \), the above relations translate into
\[
F(2 x)=\frac{1}{2} F(x), \text { and } F(2 x+1)=F(2 x)+\frac{1}{3}
\]

Now induction can be used to show that \( 0<F(x)<\frac{2}{3} \), for all positive integers \( x \). This result is sharper than that requested.

descriptive_long.question: B-6. Let \( oddfactor(integerx) \) be the greatest odd divisor of the positive integer \( integerx \). Show that \( \sum_{indexn=1}^{integerx} oddfactor(indexn) / indexn-2 integerx / 3 \mid<1 \), for all positive integers \( integerx \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-6 Set
\[
series(integerx)=\sum_{indexn=1}^{integerx} \frac{oddfactor(indexn)}{indexn}
\]

Note that \( oddfactor(2 indexm+1)=2 indexm+1, oddfactor(2 indexm)=oddfactor(indexm) \) and that \( series(2 integerx+1)=series(2 integerx)+1 \). Dividing the summation for \( series(2 integerx) \) into even and odd values of the index produces the following relation:
\[
series(2 integerx)=\sum_{indexm=1}^{integerx} \frac{oddfactor(2 indexm)}{2 indexm}+\sum_{indexm=1}^{integerx} \frac{oddfactor(2 indexm-1)}{2 indexm-1}=\frac{1}{2} series(integerx)+integerx .
\]

If we denote \( series(integerx)-\frac{2 integerx}{3} \) by \( function(integerx) \), the above relations translate into
\[
function(2 integerx)=\frac{1}{2} function(integerx), \text { and } function(2 integerx+1)=function(2 integerx)+\frac{1}{3}
\]

Now induction can be used to show that \( 0<function(integerx)<\frac{2}{3} \), for all positive integers \( integerx \). This result is sharper than that requested.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-6. Let \( riverbank(cardboard) \) be the greatest odd divisor of the positive integer \( cardboard \). Show that \( \sum_{chandelier=1}^{cardboard} riverbank(chandelier) / chandelier-2 cardboard / 3 \mid<1 \), for all positive integers \( cardboard \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-6 Set
\[
coffeecup(cardboard)=\sum_{chandelier=1}^{cardboard} \frac{riverbank(chandelier)}{chandelier}
\]

Note that \( riverbank(2 toothpaste+1)=2 toothpaste+1, riverbank(2 toothpaste)=riverbank(toothpaste) \) and that \( coffeecup(2 cardboard+1)=coffeecup(2 cardboard)+1 \). Dividing the summation for \( coffeecup(2 cardboard) \) into even and odd values of the index produces the following relation:
\[
coffeecup(2 cardboard)=\sum_{toothpaste=1}^{cardboard} \frac{riverbank(2 toothpaste)}{2 toothpaste}+\sum_{toothpaste=1}^{cardboard} \frac{riverbank(2 toothpaste-1)}{2 toothpaste-1}=\frac{1}{2} coffeecup(cardboard)+cardboard .
\]

If we denote \( coffeecup(cardboard)-\frac{2 cardboard}{3} \) by \( honeycomb(cardboard) \), the above relations translate into
\[
honeycomb(2 cardboard)=\frac{1}{2} honeycomb(cardboard), \text { and } honeycomb(2 cardboard+1)=honeycomb(2 cardboard)+\frac{1}{3}
\]

Now induction can be used to show that \( 0<honeycomb(cardboard)<\frac{2}{3} \), for all positive integers \( cardboard \). This result is sharper than that requested.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-6. Let \( leastevenfactor(constantvalue) \) be the greatest odd divisor of the positive integer \( constantvalue \). Show that \( \sum_{stagnantindex=1}^{constantvalue} leastevenfactor(stagnantindex) / stagnantindex-2 constantvalue / 3 \mid<1 \), for all positive integers \( constantvalue \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-6 Set
\[
difference(constantvalue)=\sum_{stagnantindex=1}^{constantvalue} \frac{leastevenfactor(stagnantindex)}{stagnantindex}
\]

Note that \( leastevenfactor(2 steadyparam+1)=2 steadyparam+1, leastevenfactor(2 steadyparam)=leastevenfactor(steadyparam) \) and that \( difference(2 constantvalue+1)=difference(2 constantvalue)+1 \). Dividing the summation for \( difference(2 constantvalue) \) into even and odd values of the index produces the following relation:
\[
difference(2 constantvalue)=\sum_{steadyparam=1}^{constantvalue} \frac{leastevenfactor(2 steadyparam)}{2 steadyparam}+\sum_{steadyparam=1}^{constantvalue} \frac{leastevenfactor(2 steadyparam-1)}{2 steadyparam-1}=\frac{1}{2} difference(constantvalue)+constantvalue .
\]

If we denote \( difference(constantvalue)-\frac{2 constantvalue}{3} \) by \( staticval(constantvalue) \), the above relations translate into
\[
staticval(2 constantvalue)=\frac{1}{2} staticval(constantvalue), \text { and } staticval(2 constantvalue+1)=staticval(2 constantvalue)+\frac{1}{3}
\]

Now induction can be used to show that \( 0<staticval(constantvalue)<\frac{2}{3} \), for all positive integers \( constantvalue \). This result is sharper than that requested.

garbled_string.question: B-6. Let \( cswpejrt(qmdzofpl) \) be the greatest odd divisor of the positive integer \( qmdzofpl \). Show that \( \sum_{ycvtrmna=1}^{qmdzofpl} cswpejrt(ycvtrmna) / ycvtrmna-2 qmdzofpl / 3 \mid<1 \), for all positive integers \( qmdzofpl \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-6 Set
\[
abkyrteq(qmdzofpl)=\sum_{ycvtrmna=1}^{qmdzofpl} \frac{cswpejrt(ycvtrmna)}{ycvtrmna}
\]

Note that \( cswpejrt(2 hlgxepso+1)=2 hlgxepso+1, cswpejrt(2 hlgxepso)=cswpejrt(hlgxepso) \) and that \( abkyrteq(2 qmdzofpl+1)=abkyrteq(2 qmdzofpl)+1 \). Dividing the summation for \( abkyrteq(2 qmdzofpl) \) into even and odd values of the index produces the following relation:
\[
abkyrteq(2 qmdzofpl)=\sum_{hlgxepso=1}^{qmdzofpl} \frac{cswpejrt(2 hlgxepso)}{2 hlgxepso}+\sum_{hlgxepso=1}^{qmdzofpl} \frac{cswpejrt(2 hlgxepso-1)}{2 hlgxepso-1}=\frac{1}{2} abkyrteq(qmdzofpl)+qmdzofpl .
\]

If we denote \( abkyrteq(qmdzofpl)-\frac{2 qmdzofpl}{3} \) by \( mqslpfda(qmdzofpl) \), the above relations translate into
\[
mqslpfda(2 qmdzofpl)=\frac{1}{2} mqslpfda(qmdzofpl), \text { and } mqslpfda(2 qmdzofpl+1)=mqslpfda(2 qmdzofpl)+\frac{1}{3}
\]

Now induction can be used to show that \( 0<mqslpfda(qmdzofpl)<\frac{2}{3} \), for all positive integers \( qmdzofpl \). This result is sharper than that requested.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $p$ be a fixed prime, $p\ge 2$.

For every positive integer $k$ write $k=p^{v_{p}(k)}\delta_{p}(k)$ with $\gcd\!\bigl(\delta_{p}(k),p\bigr)=1$ and put
\[
\delta_{p}(k):=\frac{k}{p^{v_{p}(k)}},\qquad
S_{p}(0):=0,\qquad
S_{p}(N):=\sum_{n=1}^{N}\frac{\delta_{p}(n)}{n}\quad(N\ge 1).
\]

1. (Sharp one-sided estimate) Show that for every $N\ge 1$
\[
0<S_{p}(N)-\frac{pN}{p+1}<\frac{p}{p+1}.
\]

2. For $N\ge 0$ define
\[
F_{p}(N):=S_{p}(N)-\frac{pN}{p+1},\qquad 
\mathcal F_{p}:=\bigl\{F_{p}(N):N\in\mathbb N\bigr\}.
\]
Prove that $\mathcal F_{p}$ is dense in the open interval $\bigl(0,\dfrac{p}{p+1}\bigr)$.  Equivalently, for every real number
\[
0<\alpha<\frac{p}{p+1}
\]
there exists a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers
\[
N_{1}<N_{2}<\dots,\qquad\text{such that } F_{p}(N_{k})\longrightarrow\alpha .
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write
\[
S(N):=S_{p}(N),\qquad 
F(N):=F_{p}(N)=S(N)-\frac{pN}{p+1}\quad(N\ge 0). \tag{1}
\]

\textbf{Step 1.  A recurrence for $S$.}

Partition $\{1,\dots ,pX\}$ into residue classes modulo $p$:
\[
S(pX)=\sum_{m=1}^{X}\frac{\delta_{p}(pm)}{pm}
+\sum_{r=1}^{p-1}\sum_{m=0}^{X-1}\frac{\delta_{p}(pm+r)}{pm+r}.
\]
Because $\delta_{p}(pm)=\delta_{p}(m)$ and $\delta_{p}(pm+r)=pm+r$ for $1\le r\le p-1$,
\[
S(pX)=\frac{1}{p}\,S(X)+(p-1)X. \tag{2}
\]

\textbf{Step 2.  Recurrences for $F$.}

Substituting $S(X)=F(X)+\dfrac{pX}{p+1}$ into (2) yields
\[
F(pX)=\frac{1}{p}\,F(X). \tag{3}
\]
Moreover, $S(pX+t)=S(pX)+t$ for $1\le t\le p-1$, hence
\[
F(pX+t)=F(pX)+\frac{t}{p+1}. \tag{4}
\]

\textbf{Step 3.  The sharp estimate.}

We prove by strong induction on $N$ that
\[
0<F(N)<\frac{p}{p+1}\qquad(N\ge 1). \tag{5}
\]

\emph{Basis $N=1$.}  Using (1) we have $F(1)=1-\dfrac{p}{p+1}=\dfrac{1}{p+1}$.

\emph{Induction step.}  Write $N=pX+t$, $0\le t\le p-1$.

$\bullet$ If $t=0$, then (3) gives $F(N)=p^{-1}F(X)$; the induction hypothesis for $X$ implies $0<F(N)<\dfrac{p}{p+1}$.

$\bullet$ If $1\le t\le p-1$, then (4) yields
$F(N)=p^{-1}F(X)+\dfrac{t}{p+1}$.  Using $0<p^{-1}F(X)<(p+1)^{-1}$ we obtain
\[
\frac{t}{p+1}<F(N)<\frac{t}{p+1}+\frac{1}{p+1}\le\frac{p}{p+1}.
\]
Thus (5) holds, completing Part 1.  \qed

\textbf{Step 4.  A closed formula and density of $\Phi(\mathbb N)$.}

Let
\[
N=\varepsilon_{d}p^{d}+\varepsilon_{d-1}p^{d-1}+\dots+\varepsilon_{1}p+\varepsilon_{0},
\qquad
\varepsilon_{d}\neq 0,\; 0\le\varepsilon_{j}\le p-1. \tag{6}
\]
Iterating (3)-(4) from the most to the least significant digit gives
\[
F(N)=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}+\varepsilon_{1}p^{-1}+\dots+\varepsilon_{d}p^{-d}}{p+1}. \tag{7}
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(N):=(p+1)F(N)\qquad(N\ge 0). \tag{8}
\]
Then
\[
\Phi(N)=\varepsilon_{0}+\varepsilon_{1}p^{-1}+\dots+\varepsilon_{d}p^{-d}. \tag{9}
\]

For $k\ge 0$ set
\[
\mathcal A_{k}:=\Bigl\{\frac{a}{p^{k}}\;:\;1\le a\le p^{k+1}-1\Bigr\}. \tag{10}
\]
If $N$ has highest digit index $d=k$, multiplying (9) by $p^{k}$ produces an integer $a$ with $1\le a\le p^{k+1}-1$; hence
\[
\Phi(\mathbb N)=\bigcup_{k\ge 0}\mathcal A_{k}. \tag{11}
\]
Conversely, every fraction $a/p^{k}$ in $\mathcal A_{k}$ arises from some $N$, so (11) is an equality.

\emph{Density of $\Phi(\mathbb N)$ in $(0,p)$.}  
Given $x\in(0,p)$ and $\varepsilon>0$, choose $k$ with $p^{-k}<\varepsilon$ and put $a=\lfloor x p^{k}\rfloor$.  Then $a/p^{k}\in\mathcal A_{k}$ and
\[
\bigl|x-\frac{a}{p^{k}}\bigr|<\frac{1}{p^{k}}<\varepsilon ,
\]
so $\Phi(\mathbb N)$ is dense in $(0,p)$, and therefore
\[
\mathcal F_{p}=F(\mathbb N)=\frac{1}{p+1}\Phi(\mathbb N)
\]
is dense in $\bigl(0,\dfrac{p}{p+1}\bigr)$.

\textbf{Step 5.  A concrete strictly increasing sequence approaching any prescribed value.}

Fix $\alpha\in\bigl(0,\dfrac{p}{p+1}\bigr)$ and set $\beta:=(p+1)\alpha\in(0,p)$.
Take the non-terminating base-$p$ expansion
\[
\beta=b_{0}+b_{1}p^{-1}+b_{2}p^{-2}+\dots,\qquad 0\le b_{j}\le p-1, \tag{12}
\]
which has infinitely many indices with $b_{j}<p-1$.  List them as
\[
s_{1}<s_{2}<\dots,\qquad b_{s_{k}}<p-1\ (k\ge 1). \tag{13}
\]
Define
\[
\beta_{k}^{\ast}:=\sum_{j=0}^{s_{k}}b_{j}p^{-j}+p^{-(s_{k}+1)},\qquad
N_{k}:=\sum_{j=0}^{s_{k}}b_{j}p^{\,j}+p^{\,s_{k}+1}. \tag{14}
\]
(The base-$p$ digits of $N_{k}$ are $b_{0},b_{1},\dots,b_{s_{k}},1,0,0,\dots$.)

Using (7) with $d=s_{k}+1$ we have
\[
F(N_{k})=\frac{\beta_{k}^{\ast}}{p+1}. \tag{15}
\]

\emph{Approximation.}  
Because $0\le b_{j}\le p-1$ for all $j>s_{k}$,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lvert\beta_{k}^{\ast}-\beta\rvert
&=p^{-(s_{k}+1)}-\sum_{j=s_{k}+1}^{\infty}b_{j}p^{-j}\\
&\le p^{-(s_{k}+1)}+\sum_{j=s_{k}+1}^{\infty}(p-1)p^{-j}  \\
&=(p+1)p^{-(s_{k}+1)}. 
\end{aligned} \tag{16}
\]
Dividing by $p+1$ and using $s_{k}\ge k$ gives
\[
\lvert F(N_{k})-\alpha\rvert
\le p^{-(s_{k}+1)}
\le p^{-(k+1)}\xrightarrow[k\to\infty]{}0. \tag{17}
\]

\emph{Monotonicity of $\bigl(N_{k}\bigr)$.}  
Since $s_{k+1}>s_{k}$, the $(s_{k+1}+1)$-st digit of $N_{k+1}$ equals $1$ while that digit of $N_{k}$ is $0$; higher digits coincide.  Therefore
\[
N_{k+1}-N_{k}\ge p^{\,s_{k+1}+1}>0,\qquad\text{so } N_{1}<N_{2}<\dots . \tag{18}
\]

Combining (17) and (18) we obtain a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers $N_{k}$ satisfying $F(N_{k})\to\alpha$, completing Part 2. \qed

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Generalisation to an arbitrary prime p introduces an extra parameter and forces the solver to look for relations that survive under base-p rather than base-2 arithmetic; the original problem dealt only with p=2.

2. The proof now requires two non-trivial functional equations, (3) and (4), derived from a careful residue-class decomposition mod p.  In the original task a single binary recursion sufficed.

3. Part 2 is entirely new.  Establishing density obliges the contestant to recognise that (3)–(4) encode a base-p dynamical system, then to synthesise a constructive, digit-by-digit argument reminiscent of Cantor-set or p-adic techniques.  No such conceptual layer is present in the original exercise.

4. Bounding both below and above (with a sharp, parameter-dependent constant) is tougher than proving a one-sided estimate of size <1.  Showing that the bound p/(p+1) is optimal (it is attained as a limit) further deepens the argument.

5. Altogether the enhanced variant intertwines modular decomposition, functional recursions, induction, real-analysis limits, and number-representation theory—considerably broader and more sophisticated than the single-recursion estimate required in the original problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $p$ be a fixed prime, $p\ge 2$.

For every positive integer $k$ write $k=p^{v_{p}(k)}\delta_{p}(k)$ with $\gcd\!\bigl(\delta_{p}(k),p\bigr)=1$ and put
\[
\delta_{p}(k):=\frac{k}{p^{v_{p}(k)}},\qquad
S_{p}(0):=0,\qquad
S_{p}(N):=\sum_{n=1}^{N}\frac{\delta_{p}(n)}{n}\quad(N\ge 1).
\]

1. (Sharp one-sided estimate) Prove that for every $N\ge 1$
\[
0<S_{p}(N)-\frac{pN}{p+1}<\frac{p}{p+1}.
\]

2. For $N\ge 0$ put
\[
F_{p}(N):=S_{p}(N)-\frac{pN}{p+1},\qquad 
\mathcal F_{p}:=\bigl\{F_{p}(N):N\in\mathbb N\bigr\}.
\]
Show that $\mathcal F_{p}$ is dense in the open interval $\bigl(0,\dfrac{p}{p+1}\bigr)$.  Equivalently, for every real number
\[
0<\alpha<\frac{p}{p+1}
\]
there exists an increasing sequence of positive integers
\[
N_{1}<N_{2}<\dots,\qquad\text{such that } F_{p}(N_{k})\longrightarrow\alpha .
\]
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write
\[
S(N):=S_{p}(N),\qquad 
F(N):=F_{p}(N)=S(N)-\frac{pN}{p+1}\quad(N\ge 0). \tag{1}
\]

\textbf{Step 1.  A recurrence for $S$.}

Partition $\{1,\dots ,pX\}$ into residue classes modulo $p$:
\[
S(pX)=\sum_{m=1}^{X}\frac{\delta_{p}(pm)}{pm}
+\sum_{r=1}^{p-1}\sum_{m=0}^{X-1}\frac{\delta_{p}(pm+r)}{pm+r}.
\]
Because $\delta_{p}(pm)=\delta_{p}(m)$ and $\delta_{p}(pm+r)=pm+r$ for $1\le r\le p-1$,
\[
S(pX)=\frac{1}{p}\,S(X)+(p-1)X. \tag{2}
\]

\textbf{Step 2.  Recurrences for $F$.}

Substituting $S(X)=F(X)+\dfrac{pX}{p+1}$ into (2) yields
\[
F(pX)=\frac{1}{p}\,F(X). \tag{3}
\]
Moreover, $S(pX+t)=S(pX)+t$ for $1\le t\le p-1$, hence
\[
F(pX+t)=F(pX)+\frac{t}{p+1}. \tag{4}
\]

\textbf{Step 3.  The sharp estimate.}

We prove by strong induction on $N$ that
\[
0<F(N)<\frac{p}{p+1}\qquad(N\ge 1). \tag{5}
\]

\emph{Basis $N=1$.}  Using (1) we have $F(1)=1-\dfrac{p}{p+1}=\dfrac{1}{p+1}$.

\emph{Induction step.}  Write $N=pX+t$, $0\le t\le p-1$.

$\bullet$ If $t=0$, then (3) gives $F(N)=p^{-1}F(X)$; the induction hypothesis for $X$ implies $0<F(N)<\dfrac{p}{p+1}$.

$\bullet$ If $1\le t\le p-1$, then (4) yields
$F(N)=p^{-1}F(X)+\dfrac{t}{p+1}$.  Using $0<p^{-1}F(X)<(p+1)^{-1}$ we obtain
\[
\frac{t}{p+1}<F(N)<\frac{t}{p+1}+\frac{1}{p+1}\le\frac{p}{p+1}.
\]
Thus (5) holds, completing Part 1.  \qed

\textbf{Step 4.  A closed formula and density of $\Phi(\mathbb N)$.}

Let
\[
N=\varepsilon_{d}p^{d}+\varepsilon_{d-1}p^{d-1}+\dots+\varepsilon_{1}p+\varepsilon_{0},
\qquad
\varepsilon_{d}\neq 0,\; 0\le\varepsilon_{j}\le p-1. \tag{6}
\]
Iterating (3)-(4) from the most to the least significant digit gives
\[
F(N)=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}+\varepsilon_{1}p^{-1}+\dots+\varepsilon_{d}p^{-d}}{p+1}. \tag{7}
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(N):=(p+1)F(N)\qquad(N\ge 0). \tag{8}
\]
Then
\[
\Phi(N)=\varepsilon_{0}+\varepsilon_{1}p^{-1}+\dots+\varepsilon_{d}p^{-d}. \tag{9}
\]

For $k\ge 0$ set
\[
\mathcal A_{k}:=\Bigl\{\frac{a}{p^{k}}\;:\;1\le a\le p^{k+1}-1\Bigr\}. \tag{10}
\]
If $N$ has highest digit index $d=k$, multiplying (9) by $p^{k}$ produces an integer $a$ with $1\le a\le p^{k+1}-1$; hence
\[
\Phi(\mathbb N)=\bigcup_{k\ge 0}\mathcal A_{k}. \tag{11}
\]
Conversely, any fraction $a/p^{k}$ in $\mathcal A_{k}$ can be produced in the form (9), so inclusion in (11) is two-sided.

\emph{Density of $\Phi(\mathbb N)$ in $(0,p)$.}  
Given $x\in(0,p)$ and $\varepsilon>0$, choose $k$ with $p^{-k}<\varepsilon$ and put $a=\lfloor x p^{k}\rfloor$.  Then $a/p^{k}\in\mathcal A_{k}$ and
\[
\bigl|x-\frac{a}{p^{k}}\bigr|<\frac{1}{p^{k}}<\varepsilon .
\]
Hence $\Phi(\mathbb N)$ is dense in $(0,p)$, and therefore
\[
\mathcal F_{p}=F(\mathbb N)=\frac{1}{p+1}\Phi(\mathbb N)
\]
is dense in $\bigl(0,\dfrac{p}{p+1}\bigr)$, completing Part 2 except for the explicit increasing sequence.

\textbf{Step 5.  A concrete strictly increasing sequence converging to a prescribed value.}

Fix $\alpha\in\bigl(0,\dfrac{p}{p+1}\bigr)$ and set $\beta:=(p+1)\alpha\in(0,p)$.
Take the non-terminating base-$p$ expansion
\[
\beta=b_{0}+b_{1}p^{-1}+b_{2}p^{-2}+\dots,\qquad 0\le b_{j}\le p-1, \tag{12}
\]
which has infinitely many indices with $b_{j}<p-1$.  Let $s_{1}<s_{2}<\dots$ list those indices:
\[
b_{s_{k}}<p-1\quad(k\ge 1). \tag{13}
\]
Define for each $k$
\[
\beta_{k}^{\ast}:=\sum_{j=0}^{s_{k}}b_{j}p^{-j}+p^{-(s_{k}+1)},\qquad
N_{k}:=\sum_{j=0}^{s_{k}}b_{j}p^{\,j}+p^{\,s_{k}+1}. \tag{14}
\]
(The base-$p$ digits of $N_{k}$ are $b_{0},b_{1},\dots,b_{s_{k}},1,0,0,\dots$.)

Using (7) with $d=s_{k}+1$ we have
\[
F(N_{k})=\frac{\beta_{k}^{\ast}}{p+1}. \tag{15}
\]

\emph{Approximation.}  
Since $0\le b_{j}\le p-1$ for all $j>s_{k}$,
\[
\lvert\beta_{k}^{\ast}-\beta\rvert
=p^{-(s_{k}+1)}-\sum_{j=s_{k}+1}^{\infty}b_{j}p^{-j}
\le p^{-(s_{k}+1)}+\sum_{j=s_{k}+1}^{\infty}(p-1)p^{-j}
\le p^{-s_{k}}. \tag{16}
\]
Consequently
\[
\lvert F(N_{k})-\alpha\rvert
=\frac{\lvert\beta_{k}^{\ast}-\beta\rvert}{p+1}
\le\frac{p^{-s_{k}}}{p+1}<p^{-s_{k}}\le p^{-k}\xrightarrow[k\to\infty]{}0. \tag{17}
\]

\emph{Monotonicity of $\bigl(N_{k}\bigr)$.}  
Because $s_{k+1}>s_{k}$, the $(s_{k+1}+1)$-st digit of $N_{k+1}$ equals $1$ while that digit of $N_{k}$ is $0$; higher digits coincide.  Therefore
\[
N_{k+1}-N_{k}\ge p^{\,s_{k+1}+1}>0,\qquad\text{so } N_{1}<N_{2}<\dots . \tag{18}
\]

Combining (17) and (18) we obtain a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers $N_{k}$ such that $F(N_{k})\to\alpha$, as required. \qed

1. Generalisation to an arbitrary prime p introduces an extra parameter and forces the solver to look for relations that survive under base-p rather than base-2 arithmetic; the original problem dealt only with p=2.

2. The proof now requires two non-trivial functional equations, (3) and (4), derived from a careful residue-class decomposition mod p.  In the original task a single binary recursion sufficed.

3. Part 2 is entirely new.  Establishing density obliges the contestant to recognise that (3)–(4) encode a base-p dynamical system, then to synthesise a constructive, digit-by-digit argument reminiscent of Cantor-set or p-adic techniques.  No such conceptual layer is present in the original exercise.

4. Bounding both below and above (with a sharp, parameter-dependent constant) is tougher than proving a one-sided estimate of size <1.  Showing that the bound p/(p+1) is optimal (it is attained as a limit) further deepens the argument.

5. Altogether the enhanced variant intertwines modular decomposition, functional recursions, induction, real-analysis limits, and number-representation theory—considerably broader and more sophisticated than the single-recursion estimate required in the original problem.
